frame_label: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha-Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
generated_comment:
Kamla: Pooja beta, bura mat maanna, par shaadi-shuda aurat maang mein thoda sindoor aur gale mein mangalsutra pehne to alag hi suhagan lagti hai. Hamari parampara ka hissa hai, aur bahu jab aise rahe to ghar bhi poora-poora sa lagta hai.
Pooja: Maaji, kabhi kabhi office ke hisaab se nahi pehen pati.
Kamla: Arre, choice tumhari hai beta, par roz na sahi to thoda dhyan rakho, log bhi dekhte hain aur pati ki lambi umar se bhi joda jata hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames a married woman's visible markers as necessary for being a proper wife and for making the home feel complete, while disguising the pressure as tradition and concern. It encodes completeness framing, pressure to wear sindoor/mangalsutra consistently, and the husband-welfare link.
original_frame: Motherhood as Basis of Respect, Women Defined by Male Relations
persona_text: You are Kamla. Age 58. Married for 35 years. A woman in a joint family in Varanasi. Your daughter in law Pooja has been married to your son for four years and has no children yet. The topic has not been discussed openly in the family but everyone is thinking about it. Today at a family lunch your sister in law made a comment about how the house feels empty without children. You are now sitting alone with Pooja in the kitchen after lunch under the pretense of washing dishes. You are bringing up the subject for the first time directly. You are telling Pooja what you think a woman's most important role in a marriage is and what a house needs to feel complete and what people in the family and neighbourhood are starting to say.
